Standard Essay Structure: Most teachers and exam prep providers recommend a 4‑paragraph structure: introduction, two body paragraphs, and conclusion. In the introduction, paraphrase the question and give aclear overall position(e.g., fully agree, mostly agree, or partially agree), which you maintain consistently throughout. Body paragraph 1 develops your strongest reason with explanation and specific, realistic examples; body paragraph 2 adds a second distinct reason or a nuanced extension of your view, again following a clear logical pattern (often PEEL: Point–Example–Explain–Link). The conclusion briefly restates your opinion and the two main supporting ideas without adding new arguments.
Tips to Achieve Band 7+: For Band 7 Task Response, you mustaddress all partsand present a clear, well‑developed position across the whole essay. Make sure each body paragraph focuses onone central idea, fully extended with explanation and specific examples rather than lists of undeveloped points. Cohesion: use referencing (“this trend”, “such measures”) and a range of linking devices, but keep them natural and not mechanical; logical progression matters more than fancy connectors. Lexical resource: show topic‑specific vocabulary (e.g., “public health campaigns”, “regulatory frameworks”) and accurate collocations, with only occasional errors. Grammar: aim for mostly error‑free complex sentences and amixof simple and complex structures rather than forcing complexity in every sentence.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Giving amixed or unclear opinionthat changes between introduction, body and conclusion, which weakens Task Response. Sitting “on the fence” without explaining how far you agree or disagree (for “to what extent” questions). Writing one long body paragraph with many underdeveloped ideas instead of two focused paragraphs with fully extended arguments. Over‑general, anecdotal, or unrealistic examples that do not clearly support the main point. Overusing memorised opinion phrases or linking words, leading to a mechanical tone and reduced coherence score. Frequent grammar slips in complex sentences (articles, subject–verb agreement, tense consistency) that lower Grammatical Range and Accuracy.

Question Type: 2. Discussion (Discuss both views and give your own opinion)
Standard Essay Structure: Typical structure is either 4 or 5 paragraphs, but always with clear separation of the two views. A common high‑band pattern: introduction (paraphrase + state that both views will be discussed + indicate your own opinion), body paragraph 1 (explain and support view A), body paragraph 2 (explain and support view B), and conclusion (restate your opinion and summarise main arguments). Some teachers recommend an additional short paragraph for your own view if you want to keep the “both views” paragraphs fully neutral, but the key is that your opinion must be obvious throughout. Each body paragraph should open with a topic sentence clearly signalling which side you are discussing.
Tips to Achieve Band 7+: Band 7 Task Response requires that youpresent both views fairlyand make your own position clear and consistent. When developing ideas, show why reasonable people might hold each view before you evaluate it; this gives the “well‑developed response” examiners expect. Use paragraphing to create a logical contrast between sides and to highlight your stance (e.g., concessive structures: “While it is true that…, I believe that…”). Cohesion: use comparative and contrastive language (“on the one hand…”, “by contrast…”, “nevertheless…”) to link arguments logically rather than just listing opinions. Lexical resource: draw on evaluative language (“compelling argument”, “significant drawback”, “long‑term implications”) and precise topic lexis. Grammar: show control of complex clauses for comparison and concession (although, whereas, even though, while).
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Ignoring one side or discussing it very briefly, which is treated asnot fully addressing the task. Mentioning your opinion only in the conclusion or changing it at the end, which makes the position unclear. Mixing both views in the same body paragraph so the reader cannot see a clear logical organisation. Over‑using memorised “on the one hand / on the other hand” without genuinely contrasting well‑developed points. Presenting one side as a caricature (weak or simplistic) instead of showing why people reasonably support it, which limits depth of development. Grammar errors in complex comparative or concessive sentences (misuse of “while”, “whereas”, or fragmenting clauses).

Standard Essay Structure: Most expert sources recommend a 4‑paragraph structure: introduction, problems/causes paragraph, solutions paragraph, conclusion. In the introduction, paraphrase the situation and state that you will discuss problems and suggest solutions (or causes and solutions, depending on wording). Body paragraph 1 identifiestwo or three key problems/causes, each explained and sometimes exemplified; body paragraph 2 proposescorresponding solutions, again explained with mechanisms and, where possible, realistic examples. The conclusion summarises main problems and evaluates which solutions are most effective or feasible.
Tips to Achieve Band 7+: Band 7 Task Response here means fully covering bothproblems and solutions(or causes and solutions) with relevant, extended ideas. Ensure a clearproblem–solution mapping: for each major problem, there is at least one logical solution, which you explain in terms of how it would work in practice. Coherence: signal the shift clearly between “problems” and “solutions” paragraphs, and within paragraphs move from general point to explanation to example. Lexical resource: use cause–effect and policy vocabulary (“underlying cause”, “exacerbate”, “implement regulations”, “public awareness campaigns”). Grammar: show control of structures that express cause, effect and purpose (“due to”, “leads to”, “in order to”, “so that”).
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Listing problems without explaining them, or proposing solutions that are vague (“the government should do more”) with no mechanism, which weakens development. Writing only about causes but not solutions (or vice versa), so the task is only partially addressed. Presenting solutions that do not logically address the stated problems (e.g., suggesting education for a purely economic cause without explanation of the link). Overloading paragraphs with too many brief points rather than 2–3 well‑extended ideas. Repeating the same solution for multiple problems without adapting the explanation, which reduces coherence and lexical range. Frequent tense and article errors when describing general processes and policies, which harms the impression of control.

Standard Essay Structure: The core pattern is again 4 paragraphs: introduction, advantages paragraph, disadvantages paragraph, conclusion. In the introduction, paraphrase and either give a neutral outline (“this essay will discuss both advantages and disadvantages”) or, for “outweigh” questions, clearly state which side is stronger. Body paragraph 1 explains 1–2 main advantages with explanation and concrete examples; body paragraph 2 does the same for disadvantages (or vice versa), keeping each paragraph focused and balanced in depth. For “outweigh” questions, many teachers recommend devoting slightly more space and detail to the side you think is stronger to make your evaluation obvious. The conclusion briefly evaluates which side dominates and why.
Tips to Achieve Band 7+: To meet Band 7 Task Response, you mustcover both advantages and disadvantages(and explicitly answer “which outweighs” if asked). Organise ideas logically: group advantages together and disadvantages together, avoiding mixing them within a single paragraph. Show depth by consideringwhobenefits or suffers (individuals, communities, governments, businesses) and short‑ vs long‑term effects. Cohesion: use comparative language to weigh sides (“although there are clear benefits…, these are outweighed by…”). Lexical resource: use evaluation and comparison vocabulary (“significant benefit”, “minor drawback”, “long‑term consequence”, “marginal gain”). Grammar: employ a range of conditional and concessive structures to compare outcomes accurately.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Failing to clearly state whether advantages or disadvantages are greater in “outweigh” questions, leaving your position ambiguous. Writing only about one side (e.g., only advantages) or giving the other side just a token sentence, which is scored as an incomplete response. Repeating the same advantage/disadvantage in different words rather than providing distinct points. Over‑reliance on simplistic pros/cons (e.g., “it is good for the economy”) without specifying mechanisms or examples. Weak paragraphing: mixing advantages and disadvantages in one paragraph or not signalling clearly when you switch from one to the other. Inaccurate comparative and superlative structures when weighing sides (“more better”, “the most importantest”), which lowers Grammar and Lexical Resource scores.

Standard Essay Structure: Official guidance and teacher sources treat this as one of the five main Task 2 types: you get a statement plustwo distinct questionsto answer. A standard high‑band structure: introduction (paraphrase + mention that you will answer both questions), body paragraph 1 fully answers question 1, body paragraph 2 fully answers question 2, and conclusion summarises your overall response and, if relevant, your evaluation. Each body paragraph should have a topic sentence explicitly linking to its specific question (“One major reason for this is…”, “Another effect of this trend is that…”).
Tips to Achieve Band 7+: The key Band 7 requirement here is toaddress all parts of the task: both questions must be answered fully and directly, with extended, supported ideas. Planning is crucial: identify the exact focus of each question (cause, effect, opinion, recommendation) and allocate a paragraph to each. Coherence: avoid blending answers together; instead, keep each paragraph tightly focused on one question so the examiner can easily follow your logic. Lexical resource: show flexibility by adapting vocabulary to the slightly different demands of each question (e.g., reasons vs consequences vs solutions). Grammar: use a variety of clause types to answer “why”, “how”, and “what” aspects precisely (relative clauses, conditionals, reason clauses).
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Answering only one of the two questions, or answering the second very briefly, which is a common cause of Band 6 in Task Response. Misreading the function of each question (e.g., giving advantages instead of explaining causes), leading to partial irrelevance. Mixing answers to both questions in the same body paragraph so that it is not clear where one response ends and the other begins. Repeating the same example or argument across both questions instead of tailoring ideas, which limits development and lexical range. Overly short or generic conclusions that do not synthesise answers to both questions. Persistent grammar errors in complex sentences when trying to respond to multi‑part prompts quickly, especially under time pressure.
